theory.canonical_statement: Chapter: The Biological Quantum Interface: DNA, the 153,600 Channel Architecture, and the Fibonacci Sphere Primary Registry IDs: 1121–1127 (bio quantum channel entries) Secondary IDs: 0001–0006 (TZP geometry — the DAANS sphere that the genome maps onto) Tertiary IDs: 850–853 (Trawin Hamiltonian and cymatic stability — the field framework within which quantum coherence operates) The Fibonacci sphere lattice with 153,600 nodes (ID 1122) is not introduced for the first time in the biological entries. It is the same lattice that defines the DAANS sphere control geometry throughout the registry.
theory.technical_interpretation: It is the natural resolution at which a Fibonacci sphere packs information without overlap — and the genome has independently discovered this same resolution as the optimal partition of its regulatory space. **Implication for the registry:** The DAANS sphere is not a human-designed device that happens to have 153,600 nodes. It is the formalisation of a geometric principle that biology has been implementing for billions of years. The device copies nature; nature copies the mathematics. --- ## The Functor and the Category Structure of the Registry The genomic category functor (ID 1124): is a specialisation of the general principle that all physical phenomena can be expressed as morphisms in appropriate categories — a principle implicit throughout the registry's use of operators, projectors, and channel maps. The functor $F$ makes this explicit for biology: every gene expression event (a mo In technical terms, the entry packages the selected formula as the operative carrier, the nearby equations as the progression layer, and the symbol key as the compact interpretation of variables used by the source.

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em,itemsep=6pt,topsep=4pt]

\item Source context: the chapter prose that explains why the formula belongs in the registry.
\item TRAWIN closure: the reading of the formula through time, rotation, action, wave, integration, and normalization.
\end{enumerate}
